questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation in C++. Which memory segment does each use?
keyPoints: Static: compile-time allocation, fixed size, uses stack memory, automatic deallocation
Dynamic: runtime allocation, variable size, uses heap memory, manual deallocation
Stack: fast but limited size; Heap: larger but slower access

questions.question: What is a memory leak? How can memory leaks be prevented in C++?
keyPoints: Memory leak: allocated memory not freed, causing resource wastage
Prevention: always pair new with delete, new[] with delete[]
Use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr)
Set pointer to nullptr after deletion

questions.question: What are dangling pointers? How can they be avoided?
keyPoints: Dangling pointer: points to freed memory
Avoid: set pointer to nullptr after delete
Avoid: ensure pointer validity before use
Avoid: be careful with pointers to local variables

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'delete' and 'delete[]' with examples.
keyPoints: delete: frees single object memory
delete[]: frees array memory, calls destructors for each element
Example: int* p = new int; delete p;
Example: int* arr = new int[10]; delete[] arr;

questions.question: What is the Rule of Three in C++? Why is it important?
keyPoints: If class has pointer to dynamic memory, must define: Destructor, Copy constructor, Copy assignment operator
Prevents memory leaks and double-free errors
Missing any leads to shallow copy problems
Modern C++: Rule of Five includes move constructor and move assignment

questions.question: What are smart pointers? Name two types and explain their differences.
keyPoints: Smart pointers: classes that automatically manage memory
unique_ptr: exclusive ownership, automatically deletes when out of scope
shared_ptr: reference counted, deletes when last reference gone
weak_ptr: non-owning reference to shared_ptr

questions.question: Compare malloc/free with new/delete in C++.
keyPoints: malloc/free: C functions, return void*, require casting, size manually specified
new/delete: C++ operators, return typed pointer, call constructors/destructors
new: throws exception on failure; malloc returns NULL
new/delete are preferred in C++ for type safety

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate dynamic memory allocation for a 2D array. Include functions to create the array, initialize it, display its contents, and properly deallocate the memory.
keyPoints: Use new to allocate rows and columns
Initialize using nested loops
Display matrix in tabular format
Free memory in reverse order: delete[] for columns first, then rows
Show complete working code with comments

questions.question: Create a class 'String' that manages a character array using dynamic memory. Implement constructor, destructor, copy constructor, and assignment operator following the Rule of Three.
keyPoints: Class should store char pointer and length
Constructor allocates memory for string
Destructor frees allocated memory
Copy constructor performs deep copy
Assignment operator checks for self-assignment and frees old memory first

questions.question: Explain the stack and heap memory segments in C++. Write a program showing variables allocated on stack vs heap, and explain the output.
keyPoints: Stack: automatic allocation, limited size, LIFO deallocation
Heap: dynamic allocation via new, larger but slower
Program demonstrates stack variable scope behavior
Program shows heap variable persisting outside function
Discuss advantages and disadvantages of each

questions.question: Write a C++ program to implement a simple dynamic array class with functions to add elements, remove elements, and display. Use new/delete for memory management.
keyPoints: Class with dynamic int array
Functions: add(element), remove(index), display()
Resize array when needed (create new, copy, delete old)
Handle edge cases: empty array, invalid index
Proper memory cleanup in destructor
